title: Headache — Red Flags
oneLiner: Red flag headaches signal serious underlying pathology (SAH, meningitis, arteritis) — miss them and you miss a life-threatening diagnosis.

sections.1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You:
**Classic vignette:**  
A 45-year-old woman presents to ED with a “thunderclap” headache that reached maximal intensity within seconds. She describes it as “the worst headache of my life.” On examination she has mild neck stiffness but no focal neurology. BP is 180/100. CT head shows subarachnoid haemorrhage.  

**Dissect the vignette:**  
- **Thunderclap onset** → abrupt peak in <1 minute = SAH until proven otherwise (classic stem clue).  
- **“Worst headache of my life”** → patient’s own description — highly specific for SAH when triggered by exertion.  
- **Mild neck stiffness** → meningeal irritation from blood in the subarachnoid space.  
- **Elevated BP** → either pre-existing hypertension (risk factor) or physiological response to pain/raised ICP.  
- **No focal neurology** → common in non-aneurysmal SAH; focal signs suggest aneurysm location (e.g., III nerve palsy with posterior communicating artery aneurysm).  

**Atypical presentations:**  
- **Older patient (>50) with scalp tenderness and jaw claudication** → think giant cell arteritis (GCA) even without visual symptoms.  
- **Headache with fever, rash, and photophobia** → meningococcal meningitis, not just viral meningitis.  
- **Headache after minor head trauma in anticoagulated patient** → subdural haematoma may present with subtle confusion and gradual headache, not thunderclap.
sections.2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Ma:
**Plain-language definition:**  
A headache is “red flag” when its features point to a dangerous secondary cause — subarachnoid haemorrhage, meningitis, cerebral venous sinus thrombosis (CVST), giant cell arteritis, or raised intracranial pressure (ICP). Primary headache disorders (migraine, tension-type, cluster) do not have red flags.  

**Why this matters:**  
- **Stakes:** Missing SAH or meningitis can be fatal within hours.  
- **Frequency:** About 1-2% of all ED headache visits have a dangerous cause; the challenge is to efficiently identify them.  
- **Common pitfalls:** Relying on CT alone to rule out SAH (misses up to 10% when done early and without CSF analysis); ignoring the “worst ever” descriptor; not asking about anticoagulation.
sections.3. Pathophysiology — The “Why” Behind Th:
**The story of a red flag headache:**  
Most dangerous headaches share a final common pathway: **nociception of intracranial pain-sensitive structures** (meninges, blood vessels, cranial nerves V, IX, X) triggered by traction, inflammation, or sudden dilation/constriction. For example:  

- **SAH:** Aneurysm rupture → arterial blood under high pressure floods the subarachnoid space → rapid rise in ICP → stretching of meningeal vessels and cranial nerves → immediate, severe headache. The blood also irritates meninges → neck stiffness and photophobia.  
- **Meningitis:** Bacterial or viral invasion → meningeal inflammation → release of inflammatory mediators (prostaglandins, cytokines) → headache, fever, neck stiffness (Kernig’s/Brudzinski’s signs), and photophobia.  
- **GCA:** Granulomatous inflammation of medium/large arteries (especially temporal artery) → vessel wall thickening → ischaemia of optic nerve (visual loss) and adjacent structures (scalp tenderness, jaw claudication). Headache is due to inflammation of the arterial wall itself.  
- **Raised ICP (tumour, abscess, hydrocephalus):** Compressive or infiltrative mass → gradual traction on pain-sensitive structures (falx, tentorium, venous sinuses) → progressive, morning-predominant headache with vomiting (vagal stimulation) and papilledema (optic nerve sheath distension).  

**Connecting to pattern recognition:**  
- Thunderclap = abrupt mechanical traction (SAH, reversible cerebral vasoconstriction syndrome)  
- Fever + neck stiffness = meningeal inflammation (meningitis)  
- Age >50 + scalp tenderness = giant cell arteritis  
- Progressive headache + vomiting + papilledema = raised ICP
sections.4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins):
| Category | Cause | Stem Clue |
|----------|-------|-----------|
| **Vascular** | Subarachnoid haemorrhage | Thunderclap, worst of life |
| | Reversible cerebral vasoconstriction syndrome (RCVS) | Thunderclap, often postpartum or with vasoactive drugs |
| | Cerebral venous sinus thrombosis (CVST) | Headache + seizures, signs of raised ICP, known prothrombotic state |
| | Giant cell arteritis | Age >50, scalp tenderness, jaw claudication, ESR>50 |
| | Carotid/vertebral artery dissection | Headache + ipsilateral Horner’s, stroke symptoms, neck trauma |
| **Infectious** | Bacterial meningitis | Fever, neck stiffness, altered GCS, petechial rash |
| | Viral meningitis / encephalitis | Fever, photophobia, CSF lymphocytosis |
| | Brain abscess | Headache + focal neurology, fever, immunocompromised |
| **Structural** | Space-occupying lesion (tumour, haematoma) | Progressive, worse in morning, vomiting, papilledema |
| | Hydrocephalus | Headache + gait disturbance, urinary incontinence (triad in NPH) |
| | Idiopathic intracranial hypertension (IIH) | Overweight young woman, headache, papilledema, visual obscurations |
| **Inflammatory** | Meningitis (bacterial/viral) – see above | |
| | Granulomatous meningitis (TB, fungus) | Subacute, immunocompromised, basilar meningeal enhancement |
| **Metabolic/Other** | Hypertensive encephalopathy (PRES) | Severe hypertension, headache, visual changes, seizures |
| | High-altitude headache | Recent ascent, other AMS symptoms |
| | Post-dural puncture headache | Positional (better lying flat), recent LP or epidural |

**Most common cause in ED:** **Migraine** is far more common than any red flag cause, but the red flag trigger is **SAH** (1% of ED headaches, but high stakes). In patients >50, **giant cell arteritis** becomes a key consideration.
sections.5. Investigations — What, Why, What It M:
**Diagnostic criteria for SAH (immediate):**  
- CT head (non-contrast) within 6 hours of onset: sensitivity >99% for SAH. After 6 hours, sensitivity drops → proceed to LP.  
- Lumbar puncture (after negative CT or if CT not done early): **xanthochromia** (bilirubin from haemoglobin breakdown) confirms SAH.  
- CT angiography if aneurysm suspected.  

**Test panel for red flag headache:**  

| Test | What it shows | Why order | How to interpret |
|------|---------------|-----------|------------------|
| **Non-contrast CT head** | Blood in subarachnoid space, mass, midline shift | Rule out SAH, tumour, IVH, hydrocephalus | Hyperdense blood in sulci/fissures = SAH; hypodensity with mass effect = tumour/abscess |
| **CT venogram** | Filling defect in dural venous sinus | Suspect CVST (headache + seizures, prothrombotic) | Empty delta sign on contrast CT = sinus thrombosis |
| **Lumbar puncture** | Opening pressure, cell count, protein, glucose, xanthochromia | Rule out SAH if CT negative; diagnose meningitis | Xanthochromia (bilirubin >0.015 AU) = SAH; WBC>1000/mcL with low glucose = bacterial meningitis |
| **ESR/CRP** | Inflammatory markers | Suspect GCA | ESR >50 mm/h (often >100) in GCA; CRP also elevated |
| **MRI brain + contrast** | Meningeal enhancement, abscess, tumour, ischaemia | When CT non-diagnostic but high suspicion | Leptomeningeal enhancement = meningitis/meningeal carcinomatosis; ring-enhancing lesion = abscess/tumour |
| **Vascular imaging (CTA/MRA)** | Aneurysm, dissection, vasoconstriction | Suspect SAH, RCVS, dissection | Aneurysm sack, string sign (dissection), “sausage-on-string” (RCVS) |

**Single finding that confirms:**  
- Xanthochromia in CSF = SAH (gold standard).  
- Blood on CT = SAH (immediate).  
- Temporal artery biopsy with granulomatous inflammation = GCA.  

**Single finding that rules out (if performed at right time):**  
- CT head within 6 hours of onset and negative = effectively rules out SAH (99.9% NPV).  
- Normal LP (no xanthochromia, normal cell count/protein) = rules out SAH and bacterial meningitis.
sections.6. Management — Approach & Reasoning:
**Immediate (first hour):**  
- **Airway, Breathing, Circulation** — always first.  
- **If SAH suspected:**  
  - CT head stat; if positive, call neurosurgery.  
  - **Nimodipine** 60 mg PO/NG every 4 hours for 21 days (prevents vasospasm).  
  - Keep BP controlled (systolic <160 mmHg) with **labetalol** 10-20 mg IV bolus or **nicardipine** infusion. Avoid excessive hypotension.  
- **If meningitis suspected:**  
  - Blood cultures + IV antibiotics immediately — do not wait for LP.  
  - **Dexamethasone** 10 mg IV q6h for 4 days (adjunctive in bacterial meningitis).  
  - **Ceftriaxone** 2 g IV q12h + **vancomycin** (if suspected resistant pneumococcus) 15-20 mg/kg IV q8h.  
- **If GCA suspected:**  
  - **Methylprednisolone** 1 g IV daily for 3 days (to protect vision), followed by oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg.  
  - Urgent temporal artery biopsy within 1 week (steroids do not affect histology for 7-10 days).  

**Ward (24-72 hours):**  
- **SAH:** Monitor in neuro ICU/ HDU. Nimodipine, BP control, EVD if hydrocephalus, repeat imaging for vasospasm (TCD).  
- **Meningitis:** Continue IV antibiotics based on cultures. Repeat LP if no improvement.  
- **GCA:** Oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg/day, taper slowly over 12-18 months. Monitor visual symptoms.  

**Long-term:**  
- **SAH survivors:** Clipping/coiling of aneurysm, then antiplatelet therapy if stented. Outpatient neuropsychology for cognitive deficits.  
- **GCA:** Low-dose prednisolone maintenance, calcium/vitamin D for osteoporosis prevention.  
- **IIH:** Weight loss, acetazolamide, LP if vision threatened.  

**What NOT to do:**  
- Do **not** give **opioids** for headache without ruling out SAH — they mask progression and cause rebound.  
- Do **not** perform LP in suspected raised ICP without imaging first (risk of brain herniation).  
- Do **not** delay antibiotics for meningitis while waiting for LP — start immediately after blood cultures.  
- Do **not** use **sumatriptan** in thunderclap headache — vasoconstriction may worsen RCVS or SAH.
sections.7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison T:
| Feature | SAH | Migraine | Meningitis | GCA |
|---------|-----|----------|------------|-----|
| **Pain** | Thunderclap (peak <1 min), severe, “worst ever” | Gradual onset (30 min-1h), throbbing, unilateral, moderate to severe | Diffuse, constant, moderate to severe, with photophobia/phonophobia | Boring, temporally located, lower intensity but persistent, with scalp tenderness |
| **Exam** | Neck stiffness, possible CN III palsy, no fever (unless late) | Normal, or focal aura (visual/scotoma) | Neck stiffness, fever, Kernig’s/Brudzinski’s, rash | Tender/temporal artery, jaw claudication, visual loss (amaurosis fugax or permanent) |
| **CT/LP** | CT: hyperdense blood in cisterns. LP: xanthochromia | Normal | CT: normal. LP: WBC >1000, low glucose, high protein | Normal (unless stroke) |
| **Labs** | Normal | Normal | Blood cultures positive, CRP↑ | ESR↑↑, CRP↑ |
| **Key diff** | Sudden onset + xanthochromia | Age <40, recurrent, triggers (food, menses) | Fever + meningism + CSF pleocytosis | Age >50, ESR >100, scalp tenderness |
sections.8. Complications — What If You Miss It?:
**Subarachnoid haemorrhage:**  
- **Rebleeding** — presents as sudden deterioration with worsening headache and coma. Requires immediate repeat imaging and neurosurgical clipping/coiling.  
- **Vasospasm → delayed cerebral ischaemia** — days 4-14 post bleed, presents with confusion, focal deficits, speech problems. Monitor with TCD; treat with nimodipine, maintain euvolemia, consider angioplasty.  
- **Hydrocephalus** — onset over days to weeks: lethargy, gait apraxia, incontinence. CT shows ventricular dilatation; EVD or VP shunt.  

**Bacterial meningitis:**  
- **Septic shock** — hypotension, multi-organ failure; manage with fluids, vasopressors, ICU.  
- **Cerebral oedema** — deteriorating GCS, Cushing’s reflex; give **mannitol 0.5-1 g/kg IV** or hypertonic saline.  
- **Stroke** — due to vasculitis (especially meningococcal); look for new focal signs.  
- **Death** — in up to 15% of bacterial cases if missed.  

**Giant cell arteritis:**  
- **Permanent vision loss** — anterior ischaemic optic neuropathy; irreversible within hours. High-dose steroids urgently.  
- **Aortic dissection/aneurysm** — late complication; monitor with imaging.  

**Late presentation patterns (the stems that test “what happens if untreated”):**  
- SAH missed → rebleed + sudden coma + fixed dilated pupil (uncal herniation).  
- Meningitis missed → DIC, purpura fulminans, adrenal haemorrhage (Waterhouse-Friderichsen syndrome in meningococcaemia).  
- GCA missed → bilateral blindness.
sections.9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls:
**Mnemonics:**  
- **SNOOP** — red flag mnemonics for headache:  
  - **S**ystemic symptoms (fever, weight loss, cancer)  
  - **N**eurologic symptoms (focal deficits, seizure)  
  - **O**nset (sudden/thunderclap)  
  - **O**lder age (>50)  
  - **P**revious headache (new type or change in pattern)  
- **POUND** for migraine (less useful for red flags, but used to differentiate):  
  - **P**ulsating, **O**ne-day duration (4-72h), **U**nilateral, **N**ausea, **D**isabling intensity (but only helps to rule out rare mimics)  

**Clinical pearls:**  
1. **Worst headache of life** is not pathognomonic — about 10% of patients with “worst ever” have SAH; the rest have migraine or other causes. But you must investigate.  
2. **CT negative for SAH does not rule it out after 6 hours** — always do an LP if suspicion remains.  
3. **Blood on CT in the interpeduncular cistern** (perimesencephalic SAH) has a benign prognosis but still requires CTA.  
4. **GCA can present without an elevated ESR** in 10% of cases — if high suspicion, treat and biopsy anyway.  
5. **Post-dural puncture headache** is positional (better lying flat) — not a red flag, but can mimic SAH. Check history of recent LP.  
6. **Coital headache** is a classic presentation of SAH – always ask if onset occurred during coitus, coughing, or straining.  
7. **Papilledema takes 24-48 hours to develop** — its absence in the first day after symptom onset does not rule out raised ICP.  

**Common mistakes to avoid:**  
- Skipping LP after negative CT in early SAH (>6 hours).  
- Treating thunderclap headache with triptans (may cause harm in RCVS/SAH).  
- Delaying steroids in GCA while waiting for biopsy — start immediately.  
- Not checking for anticoagulation use before performing LP (risk of spinal haematoma).
sections.10. Escalation Criteria:
**Call senior immediately if:**  
- GCS ≤ 14 or falling  
- Focal neurological signs (e.g., unequal pupils, limb weakness, slurred speech)  
- Seizures (especially first-ever)  
- Signs of raised ICP (papilledema, Cushing’s triad – bradycardia, hypertension, irregular breathing)  
- Suspected bacterial meningitis with shock or purpura  
- Thrombotic event suspected (CVST, dissection)  

**Specific thresholds:**  
- **NEWS2 ≥ 7** or rising trend → call MET/critical care outreach  
- **Lactate > 4 mmol/L** → suspect sepsis (meningitis/septic shock)  
- **GCS ≤ 8** → airway management and ICU referral  
- **Systolic BP > 180 mmHg with headache** → risk of hypertensive encephalopathy/SAH rebleed; call senior  
- **Temperature > 40°C** → meningitis/sepsis; start antibiotics and escalate  

**Crash team criteria:**  
- GCS 3 + no spontaneous respirations  
- Seizure >5 minutes (status epilepticus)  
- Cardiac arrest (PEA in SAH? possible rebleed)  

**Remember:**  
**Do not sign off a headache patient without asking about onset speed, age, and Neurological examination (especially fundoscopy). If in doubt, CT + LP.**

— *Senior Clinical Tutor.*

## Headache — Red Flags  
**One-liner:** Red flag headaches signal serious underlying pathology (SAH, meningitis, arteritis) — miss them and you miss a life-threatening diagnosis.  

### 1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You’ll See  
**Classic vignette:**  
A 45-year-old woman presents to ED with a “thunderclap” headache that reached maximal intensity within seconds. She describes it as “the worst headache of my life.” On examination she has mild neck stiffness but no focal neurology. BP is 180/100. CT head shows subarachnoid haemorrhage.  

**Dissect the vignette:**  
- **Thunderclap onset** → abrupt peak in <1 minute = SAH until proven otherwise (classic stem clue).  
- **“Worst headache of my life”** → patient’s own description — highly specific for SAH when triggered by exertion.  
- **Mild neck stiffness** → meningeal irritation from blood in the subarachnoid space.  
- **Elevated BP** → either pre-existing hypertension (risk factor) or physiological response to pain/raised ICP.  
- **No focal neurology** → common in non-aneurysmal SAH; focal signs suggest aneurysm location (e.g., III nerve palsy with posterior communicating artery aneurysm).  

**Atypical presentations:**  
- **Older patient (>50) with scalp tenderness and jaw claudication** → think giant cell arteritis (GCA) even without visual symptoms.  
- **Headache with fever, rash, and photophobia** → meningococcal meningitis, not just viral meningitis.  
- **Headache after minor head trauma in anticoagulated patient** → subdural haematoma may present with subtle confusion and gradual headache, not thunderclap.  

### 2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Matters)  
**Plain-language definition:**  
A headache is “red flag” when its features point to a dangerous secondary cause — subarachnoid haemorrhage, meningitis, cerebral venous sinus thrombosis (CVST), giant cell arteritis, or raised intracranial pressure (ICP). Primary headache disorders (migraine, tension-type, cluster) do not have red flags.  

**Why this matters:**  
- **Stakes:** Missing SAH or meningitis can be fatal within hours.  
- **Frequency:** About 1-2% of all ED headache visits have a dangerous cause; the challenge is to efficiently identify them.  
- **Common pitfalls:** Relying on CT alone to rule out SAH (misses up to 10% when done early and without CSF analysis); ignoring the “worst ever” descriptor; not asking about anticoagulation.  

### 3. Pathophysiology — The “Why” Behind The Findings  
**The story of a red flag headache:**  
Most dangerous headaches share a final common pathway: **nociception of intracranial pain-sensitive structures** (meninges, blood vessels, cranial nerves V, IX, X) triggered by traction, inflammation, or sudden dilation/constriction. For example:  

- **SAH:** Aneurysm rupture → arterial blood under high pressure floods the subarachnoid space → rapid rise in ICP → stretching of meningeal vessels and cranial nerves → immediate, severe headache. The blood also irritates meninges → neck stiffness and photophobia.  
- **Meningitis:** Bacterial or viral invasion → meningeal inflammation → release of inflammatory mediators (prostaglandins, cytokines) → headache, fever, neck stiffness (Kernig’s/Brudzinski’s signs), and photophobia.  
- **GCA:** Granulomatous inflammation of medium/large arteries (especially temporal artery) → vessel wall thickening → ischaemia of optic nerve (visual loss) and adjacent structures (scalp tenderness, jaw claudication). Headache is due to inflammation of the arterial wall itself.  
- **Raised ICP (tumour, abscess, hydrocephalus):** Compressive or infiltrative mass → gradual traction on pain-sensitive structures (falx, tentorium, venous sinuses) → progressive, morning-predominant headache with vomiting (vagal stimulation) and papilledema (optic nerve sheath distension).

- Thunderclap = abrupt mechanical traction (SAH, reversible cerebral vasoconstriction syndrome)  
- Fever + neck stiffness = meningeal inflammation (meningitis)  
- Age >50 + scalp tenderness = giant cell arteritis  
- Progressive headache + vomiting + papilledema = raised ICP  

### 4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins)  

| Category | Cause | Stem Clue |
|----------|-------|-----------|
| **Vascular** | Subarachnoid haemorrhage | Thunderclap, worst of life |
| | Reversible cerebral vasoconstriction syndrome (RCVS) | Thunderclap, often postpartum or with vasoactive drugs |
| | Cerebral venous sinus thrombosis (CVST) | Headache + seizures, signs of raised ICP, known prothrombotic state |
| | Giant cell arteritis | Age >50, scalp tenderness, jaw claudication, ESR>50 |
| | Carotid/vertebral artery dissection | Headache + ipsilateral Horner’s, stroke symptoms, neck trauma |
| **Infectious** | Bacterial meningitis | Fever, neck stiffness, altered GCS, petechial rash |
| | Viral meningitis / encephalitis | Fever, photophobia, CSF lymphocytosis |
| | Brain abscess | Headache + focal neurology, fever, immunocompromised |
| **Structural** | Space-occupying lesion (tumour, haematoma) | Progressive, worse in morning, vomiting, papilledema |
| | Hydrocephalus | Headache + gait disturbance, urinary incontinence (triad in NPH) |
| | Idiopathic intracranial hypertension (IIH) | Overweight young woman, headache, papilledema, visual obscurations |
| **Inflammatory** | Meningitis (bacterial/viral) – see above | |
| | Granulomatous meningitis (TB, fungus) | Subacute, immunocompromised, basilar meningeal enhancement |
| **Metabolic/Other** | Hypertensive encephalopathy (PRES) | Severe hypertension, headache, visual changes, seizures |
| | High-altitude headache | Recent ascent, other AMS symptoms |
| | Post-dural puncture headache | Positional (better lying flat), recent LP or epidural |

### 5. Investigations — What, Why, What It Means  

**Diagnostic criteria for SAH (immediate):**

| Test | What it shows | Why order | How to interpret |
|------|---------------|-----------|------------------|
| **Non-contrast CT head** | Blood in subarachnoid space, mass, midline shift | Rule out SAH, tumour, IVH, hydrocephalus | Hyperdense blood in sulci/fissures = SAH; hypodensity with mass effect = tumour/abscess |
| **CT venogram** | Filling defect in dural venous sinus | Suspect CVST (headache + seizures, prothrombotic) | Empty delta sign on contrast CT = sinus thrombosis |
| **Lumbar puncture** | Opening pressure, cell count, protein, glucose, xanthochromia | Rule out SAH if CT negative; diagnose meningitis | Xanthochromia (bilirubin >0.015 AU) = SAH; WBC>1000/mcL with low glucose = bacterial meningitis |
| **ESR/CRP** | Inflammatory markers | Suspect GCA | ESR >50 mm/h (often >100) in GCA; CRP also elevated |
| **MRI brain + contrast** | Meningeal enhancement, abscess, tumour, ischaemia | When CT non-diagnostic but high suspicion | Leptomeningeal enhancement = meningitis/meningeal carcinomatosis; ring-enhancing lesion = abscess/tumour |
| **Vascular imaging (CTA/MRA)** | Aneurysm, dissection, vasoconstriction | Suspect SAH, RCVS, dissection | Aneurysm sack, string sign (dissection), “sausage-on-string” (RCVS) |

- Xanthochromia in CSF = SAH (gold standard).  
- Blood on CT = SAH (immediate).  
- Temporal artery biopsy with granulomatous inflammation = GCA.  

**Single finding that rules out (if performed at right time):**  
- CT head within 6 hours of onset and negative = effectively rules out SAH (99.9% NPV).  
- Normal LP (no xanthochromia, normal cell count/protein) = rules out SAH and bacterial meningitis.  

### 6. Management — Approach & Reasoning  

**Immediate (first hour):**  
- **Airway, Breathing, Circulation** — always first.  
- **If SAH suspected:**  
  - CT head stat; if positive, call neurosurgery.  
  - **Nimodipine** 60 mg PO/NG every 4 hours for 21 days (prevents vasospasm).  
  - Keep BP controlled (systolic <160 mmHg) with **labetalol** 10-20 mg IV bolus or **nicardipine** infusion. Avoid excessive hypotension.  
- **If meningitis suspected:**  
  - Blood cultures + IV antibiotics immediately — do not wait for LP.  
  - **Dexamethasone** 10 mg IV q6h for 4 days (adjunctive in bacterial meningitis).  
  - **Ceftriaxone** 2 g IV q12h + **vancomycin** (if suspected resistant pneumococcus) 15-20 mg/kg IV q8h.  
- **If GCA suspected:**  
  - **Methylprednisolone** 1 g IV daily for 3 days (to protect vision), followed by oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg.  
  - Urgent temporal artery biopsy within 1 week (steroids do not affect histology for 7-10 days).  

**Ward (24-72 hours):**  
- **SAH:** Monitor in neuro ICU/ HDU. Nimodipine, BP control, EVD if hydrocephalus, repeat imaging for vasospasm (TCD).  
- **Meningitis:** Continue IV antibiotics based on cultures. Repeat LP if no improvement.  
- **GCA:** Oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg/day, taper slowly over 12-18 months. Monitor visual symptoms.  

**Long-term:**  
- **SAH survivors:** Clipping/coiling of aneurysm, then antiplatelet therapy if stented. Outpatient neuropsychology for cognitive deficits.  
- **GCA:** Low-dose prednisolone maintenance, calcium/vitamin D for osteoporosis prevention.  
- **IIH:** Weight loss, acetazolamide, LP if vision threatened.

- Do **not** give **opioids** for headache without ruling out SAH — they mask progression and cause rebound.

- Do **not** delay antibiotics for meningitis while waiting for LP — start immediately after blood cultures.  
- Do **not** use **sumatriptan** in thunderclap headache — vasoconstriction may worsen RCVS or SAH.  

### 7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison Table  

| Feature | SAH | Migraine | Meningitis | GCA |
|---------|-----|----------|------------|-----|
| **Pain** | Thunderclap (peak <1 min), severe, “worst ever” | Gradual onset (30 min-1h), throbbing, unilateral, moderate to severe | Diffuse, constant, moderate to severe, with photophobia/phonophobia | Boring, temporally located, lower intensity but persistent, with scalp tenderness |
| **Exam** | Neck stiffness, possible CN III palsy, no fever (unless late) | Normal, or focal aura (visual/scotoma) | Neck stiffness, fever, Kernig’s/Brudzinski’s, rash | Tender/temporal artery, jaw claudication, visual loss (amaurosis fugax or permanent) |
| **CT/LP** | CT: hyperdense blood in cisterns. LP: xanthochromia | Normal | CT: normal. LP: WBC >1000, low glucose, high protein | Normal (unless stroke) |
| **Labs** | Normal | Normal | Blood cultures positive, CRP↑ | ESR↑↑, CRP↑ |
| **Key diff** | Sudden onset + xanthochromia | Age <40, recurrent, triggers (food, menses) | Fever + meningism + CSF pleocytosis | Age >50, ESR >100, scalp tenderness |

### 8. Complications — What If You Miss It?  

**Subarachnoid haemorrhage:**  
- **Rebleeding** — presents as sudden deterioration with worsening headache and coma. Requires immediate repeat imaging and neurosurgical clipping/coiling.  
- **Vasospasm → delayed cerebral ischaemia** — days 4-14 post bleed, presents with confusion, focal deficits, speech problems. Monitor with TCD; treat with nimodipine, maintain euvolemia, consider angioplasty.  
- **Hydrocephalus** — onset over days to weeks: lethargy, gait apraxia, incontinence. CT shows ventricular dilatation; EVD or VP shunt.  

**Bacterial meningitis:**  
- **Septic shock** — hypotension, multi-organ failure; manage with fluids, vasopressors, ICU.  
- **Cerebral oedema** — deteriorating GCS, Cushing’s reflex; give **mannitol 0.5-1 g/kg IV** or hypertonic saline.  
- **Stroke** — due to vasculitis (especially meningococcal); look for new focal signs.  
- **Death** — in up to 15% of bacterial cases if missed.  

**Giant cell arteritis:**  
- **Permanent vision loss** — anterior ischaemic optic neuropathy; irreversible within hours. High-dose steroids urgently.  
- **Aortic dissection/aneurysm** — late complication; monitor with imaging.  

**Late presentation patterns (the stems that test “what happens if untreated”):**  
- SAH missed → rebleed + sudden coma + fixed dilated pupil (uncal herniation).  
- Meningitis missed → DIC, purpura fulminans, adrenal haemorrhage (Waterhouse-Friderichsen syndrome in meningococcaemia).  
- GCA missed → bilateral blindness.  

### 9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls  

**Mnemonics:**  
- **SNOOP** — red flag mnemonics for headache:  
  - **S**ystemic symptoms (fever, weight loss, cancer)  
  - **N**eurologic symptoms (focal deficits, seizure)  
  - **O**nset (sudden/thunderclap)  
  - **O**lder age (>50)  
  - **P**revious headache (new type or change in pattern)  
- **POUND** for migraine (less useful for red flags, but used to differentiate):  
  - **P**ulsating, **O**ne-day duration (4-72h), **U**nilateral, **N**ausea, **D**isabling intensity (but only helps to rule out rare mimics)  

**Clinical pearls:**  
1. **Worst headache of life** is not pathognomonic — about 10% of patients with “worst ever” have SAH; the rest have migraine or other causes. But you must investigate.  
2. **CT negative for SAH does not rule it out after 6 hours** — always do an LP if suspicion remains.  
3. **Blood on CT in the interpeduncular cistern** (perimesencephalic SAH) has a benign prognosis but still requires CTA.  
4. **GCA can present without an elevated ESR** in 10% of cases — if high suspicion, treat and biopsy anyway.  
5. **Post-dural puncture headache** is positional (better lying flat) — not a red flag, but can mimic SAH. Check history of recent LP.  
6. **Coital headache** is a classic presentation of SAH – always ask if onset occurred during coitus, coughing, or straining.  
7. **Papilledema takes 24-48 hours to develop** — its absence in the first day after symptom onset does not rule out raised ICP.  

**Common mistakes to avoid:**  
- Skipping LP after negative CT in early SAH (>6 hours).  
- Treating thunderclap headache with triptans (may cause harm in RCVS/SAH).  
- Delaying steroids in GCA while waiting for biopsy — start immediately.  
- Not checking for anticoagulation use before performing LP (risk of spinal haematoma).  

### 10. Escalation Criteria  

**Call senior immediately if:**  
- GCS ≤ 14 or falling  
- Focal neurological signs (e.g., unequal pupils, limb weakness, slurred speech)  
- Seizures (especially first-ever)  
- Signs of raised ICP (papilledema, Cushing’s triad – bradycardia, hypertension, irregular breathing)  
- Suspected bacterial meningitis with shock or purpura  
- Thrombotic event suspected (CVST, dissection)  

**Specific thresholds:**  
- **NEWS2 ≥ 7** or rising trend → call MET/critical care outreach  
- **Lactate > 4 mmol/L** → suspect sepsis (meningitis/septic shock)  
- **GCS ≤ 8** → airway management and ICU referral  
- **Systolic BP > 180 mmHg with headache** → risk of hypertensive encephalopathy/SAH rebleed; call senior  
- **Temperature > 40°C** → meningitis/sepsis; start antibiotics and escalate  

**Crash team criteria:**  
- GCS 3 + no spontaneous respirations  
- Seizure >5 minutes (status epilepticus)  
- Cardiac arrest (PEA in SAH? possible rebleed)  

**Remember:**  
**Do not sign off a headache patient without asking about onset speed, age, and Neurological examination (especially fundoscopy). If in doubt, CT + LP.**

— *Senior Clinical Tutor.*
